query: dupilumab FDA approval for atopic dermatitis

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is a human monoclonal antibody administered subcutaneously to inhibit the interleukin-4 receptor subunit α (IL-4R α). The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved dupilumab in April 2017 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis (AD) in adults and pediatric patients aged 6 and older, as well as for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP) in adults. [1] Since the incidence of atopic dermatitis has been rising, especially in the developed and industrialized parts of the world, dermatologists must prescribe treatment regimens that are both effective and safe to use in the long-term management of this chronic skin condition. [2] Dupilumab is also used in treating adults with atopic dermatitis symptoms inadequately controlled with topical corticosteroid use. [3] [4] Currently, no specific guidelines deem the drug safe for children younger than 6. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- Non-FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-FDA-approved indications include allergic contact dermatitis, hand dermatitis, chronic spontaneous urticaria, and alopecia areata. [1] [14] Extensive literature suggests the efficacy of dupilumab in treating conditions characterized by Th2-mediated inflammation. [15] Clinical investigations are currently being conducted to assess the effectiveness of dupilumab in treating autoimmune blistering diseases, including bullous pemphigoid and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Apply evidence-based guidelines and data to guide dupilumab treatment decisions in clinical practice. Identify the appropriate indications for dupilumab in the treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. Assess the treatment response and monitor for adverse effects during dupilumab therapy. Implement effective collaboration among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from dupilumab. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2022, dupilumab became the first treatment to receive FDA approval for prurigo nodularis (PN) in adults. [9] [10] Dupilumab is also the first biologic approved by the FDA for eosinophilic esophagitis in children older than 12. [11] [12] Dupilumab is recommended for treating eosinophilic esophagitis by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology and the American Gastroenterological Association. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is also now FDA-approved for prurigo nodularis and eosinophilic esophagitis. Non-FDA-approved indications include allergic contact dermatitis, hand dermatitis, chronic spontaneous urticaria, and alopecia areata. Extensive literature suggests the efficacy of dupilumab in treating conditions characterized by Th2-mediated inflammation. Clinical trials are currently in the pipeline to explore the potential of dupilumab in treating autoimmune blistering diseases, such as bullous pemphigoid. This activity describes dupilumab, including its mechanism of action, adverse event and safety profiles, eligible patient populations, and contraindications. It also highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing patients on dupilumab treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms, Strengths, and Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: More recently, 2 large phase 3 trials, including adults with symptoms inadequately suppressed by topical medications commonly used to manage atopic dermatitis, demonstrated the role of dupilumab in treating the condition. Patients were treated with either a placebo or 300 mg dupilumab administered weekly or every 2 weeks over 16 weeks. A significant reduction of 75% in eczema area and severity index (EASI) was observed in patients on dupilumab in both weekly and 2-weekly interval dosing regimens. [22] The FDA-approved dosage of dupilumab for specific indications is listed below. Atopic dermatitis: The suggested initial dose of dupilumab is 600 mg (administered as 2 300 mg injections), followed by a subsequent maintenance dose of 300 mg every other week (Q2W).

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is an injectable prescription medication administered subcutaneously to treat severe and refractory forms of atopic dermatitis. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the medication in April 2017 to treat moderate-to-severe, resistant, atopic dermatitis unresponsive to conventional therapy, including topical corticosteroids. Dupilumab is also used as a maintenance treatment for asthma and chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis in adults and children, in conjunction with other medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: Dupilumab is an FDA-approved medication for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis in patients 6 months and older. It is also approved as an add-on maintenance treatment for moderate-to-severe asthma in pediatric patients aged 6 and older with oral corticosteroid dependence or presenting with an eosinophilic phenotype. [26] Additionally, dupilumab is approved for eosinophilic esophagitis in patients weighing at least 40 kg (12 years or older). However, dupilumab is not FDA-approved for pediatric use in chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis or prurigo nodularis.

retrieved_docs.title: Atopic Dermatitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A newly FDA-approved biologic therapy is dupilumab, which is a monoclonal antibody that blocks the IL-4 receptor and thus the effect of IL-4 and IL-13.  Other complementary therapies include bleach baths (0.5 cup bleach in full 40 gallon tub) one to two times weekly to decrease S. aureus colonization, low allergen maternal diets during breastfeeding, and probiotic and prebiotic use in pregnant mothers and at-risk infants which has shown 50% decreased frequency of atopic dermatitis at ages 1 to 4 years old compared to placebo. [10] [11] [12] Recently Crisaborole topic ointment was approved for mild to moderate AD. The drug is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor and shown to improve skin symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is an effective and safe immunomodulating therapy for treating moderate-to-severe symptoms associated with atopic dermatitis, including itching, poor sleep quality, anxiety, and depression. Evidence from increasing trials suggests that the benefits of dupilumab far outweigh its side effects. Since no concrete literature-based evidence suggests that dupilumab is contraindicated during pregnancy or lactation, shared decision-making among healthcare team members and patients is paramount for ensuring safe and effective treatment plans. Patients should receive significant education on their treatment options, including discussing the benefits and risks to maternal and fetal health, and that patient values and preferences are considered. Consultation with an immunologist is required before initiating dupilumab for severe asthma. Moderate to severe atopic dermatitis is best managed in consultation with a dermatologist. Otorhinolaryngologist consultation is necessary for patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis. Both pharmacists and nurses need easy and direct access to the prescriber so any patient concerns regarding dupilumab use can be addressed immediately. Communication among healthcare team members is pivotal to obtaining optimal therapeutic results for dupilumab use with minimal adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Geriatric patients: Analysis of 4 RCT studies on dupilumab has revealed that patients over 60 suffering from moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis demonstrated a significant improvement in AD signs and symptoms. These results are comparable to those reported in patients under 60 years. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody of the immunoglobulin G4 subclass designed to inhibit receptor signaling downstream of the JAK-STAT pathway by blocking interleukin-4/interleukin-13 (IL-4/IL-13) receptors. [4] As an IL-4Rα antagonist, dupilumab inhibits the signaling of pro-inflammatory cytokines, or interleukins, that induce inflammatory and immunological responses in several conditions, including eczema, asthma, allergic reactions, and rhinosinusitis. [18] Essentially, IL-4Rα is a component common to both IL-4 and IL-13 receptor complexes and is ubiquitously expressed in innate and adaptive immune cells to promote the signaling of IL-4 and IL-13. [19] [20] By blocking this pathway, dupilumab affects 3 integral disease mechanisms of atopic dermatitis: the decrease of skin barrier function, the class switch to IgE, and the Th2 differentiation. [4] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Several contraindications have been noted in the literature, some of which are listed below. However, insufficient scientific data or research-based evidence supports most of these contraindications. The only FDA-labeled contraindication to using dupilumab is hypersensitivity to any of its excipients. Angioedema due to dupilumab has been reported. [34]  Dupilumab is contraindicated in patients with hamster protein hypersensitivity, as this drug is produced in Chinese hamster ovary cells. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash and allergic conjunctivitis, have occurred after dupilumab administration. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Administration -- Atopic Dermatitis
retrieved_docs.content: For pediatric patients aged 12 years and older and weighing at least 40 kg, the recommended daily dosage is 15 mg once a day. If the therapeutic response is not reached, the dose can be increased to 30 mg daily. If the desired therapeutic response is not achieved with 30 mg daily, upadacitinib should be discontinued.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: According to the American College of Rheumatology guidelines, omalizumab can be used to treat antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis. [3] In addition, other off-label uses of omalizumab include bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, keratoconjunctivitis, drug and food allergies, eosinophilic otitis media, bullous pemphigoid, and atopic dermatitis. [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatologické látky constitute a broad pharmacological category that includes keratolytika as a direct subclass, as well as the agents flutikason and furokumariny, which are themselves types of dermatologické látky. In addition to their dermatological applications, flutikason is classified therapeutically under látky ovlivňující dýchací systém, linking it to respiratory‑system pharmacology. Almitrin is also an isa member of látky ovlivňující dýchací systém, indicating its primary effect on the respiratory tract. Látky ovlivňující dýchací systém are positioned within the hierarchy of systemic antihistamines, as antihistamines for systemic use are defined by an inverse_isa relationship to this class; consequently, respiratory‑system agents are considered a specialized subset of systemic antihistamines. Phenothiazine derivatives, systemic antihistamines are themselves an isa subclass of antihistamines for systemic use, further illustrating the layered organization of antihistaminic therapies. The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab each have the therapeutic class látky ovlivňující dýchací systém, and they are all members of the ATC group other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in atc. This group is itself related by inverse_isa to other respiratory system products in atc, situating these biologics among a wider set of respiratory medications. Together, these relationships demonstrate how certain dermatological compounds (such as flutikason) intersect with respiratory therapeutics, how respiratory‑acting agents are integrated within the broader framework of systemic antihistamines, and how a specific ATC category unites several monoclonal antibody therapies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab) used to treat obstructive airway diseases. Understanding these interconnections supports comprehensive patient care across dermatology and respiratory medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions, such as severe asthma, and they share a common therapeutic classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1147
retrieved_docs.content: its topical uses, ketoconazole has important drug‑interaction considerations. The medication classes dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are contraindicated when ketoconazole is present, reflecting a risk of adverse pharmacologic interaction. Likewise, the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist ubrogepant is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, underscoring the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication list before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products. Recognizing both the extensive range of ketoconazole‑based dermatologic therapies and its contraindications with specific systemic agents is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: Other antifungals for topical use in ATC is a pharmacological class that comprises the agents butenafine and tolnaftát. Both butenafine and tolnaftát are topical antifungal medications, and each is contraindicated in patients with the hypersensitivity condition described as فرط تحسس. Recognizing that these two drugs share the same contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2484
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoprofen and butenafine are both contraindicated in the presence of hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Use of fenoprofen is not recommended for patients who have a hypersensitivity reaction, and similarly, butenafine should be avoided in individuals with hypersensitivity disease. Recognizing hypersensitivity as a contraindicating factor for these medications is essential for safe prescribing and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1275
retrieved_docs.content: Almitrin, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as agents that affect the respiratory system (“látky ovlivňující dýchací systém”). Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab each belong to this therapeutic class and are further grouped within the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC”. The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” itself is a subclass of the broader ATC group “other respiratory system products in ATC”. Together, these classifications indicate that the four biologic drugs are systemic treatments for obstructive airway diseases such as asthma, and they are organized under consistent pharmacological categories that facilitate prescribing, regulation and clinical understanding of respiratory‑targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”. These agents are used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, and they represent a distinct therapeutic class within the broader ATC framework. Fenspiride is listed both as a member of this systemic group and as a member of the inhalant group “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, indicating that it can be administered by inhalation as well as having systemic activity. Within the inhalant category, the subclass “Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excl. corticosteroids” is defined as a type of “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, highlighting non‑corticosteroid antiallergic inhaled therapies. This classification clarifies the relationships among systemic biologics, inhaled agents, and specific antiallergic inhalants, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments based on route of administration and pharmacologic class.
